examples: Real-time stats (334 features, 755 edges) and namespace breakdowns provide immediate context for scale and complexity.
Authentication/authorization metadata (e.g., “requires admin”, “token-bound”) is absent — a major gap for security analysis or exploit development.
No visible sorting controls (alphabetical, by address, by size) — critical for RE when scanning large binaries or comparing symbol tables.
Missing critical RE columns: no visible address (VA/RVA), size, or entropy/entropy-based tags — essential for function boundary analysis and obfuscation detection.
No visible column customization or export capability — limits workflow integration with external tools (e.g., IDA, Ghidra, custom scripts).
No visible memory map, section headers, or entropy analysis — missing foundational ELF introspection that RE pros expect at first glance.
Add a “Quick Insights” panel showing section headers, entry point, and entropy heatmap — this would instantly signal “serious tool” to a low-level RE professional.
No visible shape coding — all nodes appear as dots or small circles; adding shapes (e.g., squares for namespaces, diamonds for state machines) would improve accessibility and reduce cognitive load.
No branch target visualization or control flow graph — the “graph” appears to be a high-level feature/namespace dependency map, not a CFG.
Symbolic resolution and library call identification are not shown — no indication of imported/exported symbols, libc calls, or PLT/GOT entries.
Metadata summary at bottom (T0/T1/T2, namespaces, features, edges) provides quick statistical context — useful for triage.
Feature-rich metadata panel (namespaces, connections, stats) provides deep context once the user knows what to look for.
“6/334 in scene” is cryptic — doesn’t explain why only 6 are shown or how to reveal more. No “load more” or “expand all” affordance.
Metadata summary at bottom (features, edges, namespaces) gives immediate context — useful for triage.
Replace “6/334 in scene” with “Showing 6 of 334 features — click to expand full graph” and add a “+ Load All” button near the viewport controls.
No visual indicators for dangerous patterns (e.g., buffer ops, unvalidated args, hardcoded creds) — misses opportunity to flag potential bugs proactively.
Add a “VULN HINTS” toggle that overlays icons or colors on functions matching known dangerous patterns (e.g., strcpy, system, recv with no length check).
No Python pseudocode is visible — the core requirement of this evaluation is unmet. The interface shows no function-level logic translation.
“SOURCE PATTERNS (0 available)” implies the tool hasn’t extracted or rendered any function logic at all — critical for educational use.
The UI prioritizes graph topology over code semantics; without pseudocode, it’s useless for teaching binary logic to Python-savvy learners.
Visual indicators (T0/T1/T2, confidence %) provide quick risk/complexity heuristics — helpful for prioritizing analysis.
Integrate a “Teach Me” mode that auto-generates annotated pseudocode for top features (e.g., “request_permission”) with links to CTF-style explanations.
Introduce edge bundling and node clustering by namespace or function type to reduce visual clutter and improve readability of large graphs.
No visible parameter types, request/response schemas, or calling conventions for RPC nodes — critical for interface reconstruction or fuzzing.
Add a “Node Details” panel that expands on selected RPC nodes to show: parameter types, return schema, calling convention (e.g., sysv, fastcall), and associated function addresses or symbol names.
No address/offset display per node — makes it hard to correlate with disassemblers or debuggers.
Add a “Sort By” dropdown (Address, Name, Size, Type) and toggle for “Raw ELF View” to expose actual section/symbol table structure.
Include virtual address (VA) or offset in node labels (e.g., “daemon.* namespace @ 0x401000”) to enable cross-referencing with IDA/Ghidra.
No low-level binary format details visible: no field type annotations (uint32_t, char*, etc.), no byte sizes, no endianness, no raw offsets — critical for actual reverse engineering.
The “DROID” panel shows aggregated stats (334 features, 755 edges) but lacks structural breakdowns — no structs, no memory layout, no disassembly or hex view.
Bitfields, unions, or alignment padding are not represented — making it impossible to reconstruct or patch binary structures accurately.
Visual graph rendering with node types (rpc, event, schema) and namespace grouping offers a macro-level structural view — valuable for initial reconnaissance.
Add a “Struct Viewer” pane that displays parsed ELF structures with field names, types, sizes, offsets, and endianness — similar to Ghidra’s Data Type Manager or IDA’s struct editor.
Legend is minimal and lacks hover tooltips or interactive filtering — in complex graphs, users need to dynamically isolate node types, which isn’t evident here.
Introduce shape + color coding for node types (e.g., hexagons for namespaces, rounded rectangles for state machines) to support colorblind users and improve visual parsing.
Add an interactive legend with toggle buttons to show/hide node types — this would allow analysts to progressively focus on relevant subsystems (e.g., “show only cli commands and state machines”).
No field offsets, sizes, or type definitions shown for selected nodes — hides the structural details that define binary behavior.
Metadata summary (T0/T1/T2, confidence, ELF type, feature counts) is well-organized and immediately actionable for triage.
Display field metadata (offset, size, type, alignment) in the detail panel when a symbol, struct, or section is selected — this is the bread and butter of reverse engineering.
“Click to select node” instructions are buried in header; no visual cue or tooltip to explain interaction model.
No linkage between RPC endpoints and their implementing functions (e.g., no symbol resolution or cross-references to disassembly).
Information density is low in the central pane; lacks disassembly, hex dump, or symbol table views that are standard in professional RE tools.
Integrate a hex/assembly view toggle that synchronizes with selected nodes — allowing analysts to jump from high-level graph to low-level bytes instantly.
No visible assembly view whatsoever — critical omission for any professional RE tool. No mnemonics, no operands, no hex addresses, no opcode bytes.
Add a disassembly pane (Intel syntax by default) with:
Integrate a CFG view toggle that overlays assembly with control flow edges — essential for understanding logic flow in obfuscated or packed binaries.
No raw data view (hex, disassembly, or JSON dump) — critical for RE work where byte-level inspection is non-negotiable.
Add a “Raw View” toggle (hex + disassembly + JSON) for any selected node — mandatory for professional RE.
Add a “Pseudocode View” toggle that renders each selected function as Python-like pseudocode with inline comments explaining assembly idioms (e.g., “# equivalent to cmp eax, 0; jz label”).
Empty central graph viewport on first load is confusing — no visual feedback or “no nodes selected” prompt; feels broken or incomplete.
Graph viewport is empty on load — no immediate visual representation of control flow, data flow, or call graph, which is critical for RE workflow (unlike IDA/Ghidra’s disassembly view on open).
Replace the blank graph viewport with a default disassembly or function graph view (even if simplified) to immediately convey binary structure.
The graph canvas is currently empty (6/334 nodes in scene) — this makes it impossible to evaluate spatial layout, clustering, or edge density, which are critical for assessing graph usability in RE.
Empty graph on first load is confusing — no visual feedback or “getting started” prompt to explain what the user should do next.
The central graph canvas is empty on first load — no visual scaffolding or “hello world” example to demonstrate what a populated graph looks like. This risks confusion for beginners.
“ANALYSIS” tab is active by default, but the graph is empty — no indication that the user must first select a binary or trigger a render. This breaks the “overview → detail” flow.
“Start Exploring” screen is empty by default — no auto-highlighted risky functions or entry points (main, signal handlers, network binds) to jumpstart analysis.
Integrate a “Fuzz Template Generator” button that auto-generates AFL++ or libfuzzer harness stubs based on the selected RPC’s inferred interface — turning analysis into actionable tooling.
Introduce a “Export to CSV/JSON” button with column selection and filtering persistence — critical for batch analysis and correlation with other datasets.
Add a subtle “spinning node” or “pulse animation” on the selected binary (e.g., “droid”) when clicked, followed by a radial “bloom” of connected nodes to visually tie action to result.
No explicit “Find Vulnerable Functions” or “Highlight Input Handlers” toggle — forces manual exploration even for common patterns (e.g., strcpy, recv, fopen).
Auto-load and highlight the “main” function + any network-related functions (bind, listen, accept) on first load — gives immediate attack surface context without clicking “Show Namespaces.”
“+ INGEST BINARY” button is buried; no onboarding or “Start Here” cue for new users — reduces discoverability.
Add a “Welcome Graph” or “Sample Analysis” on first load showing a minimal connected graph with tooltips explaining interactions (click, double-click, right-click).
No onboarding tour, tooltips, or “CTF Beginner Mode” toggle — critical for educational accessibility.
Introduce a “Beginner Mode” toggle that simplifies the UI, highlights key features, and replaces advanced queries with “What is this binary doing?” or “Find the main function.”
No visible toggle between layout algorithms (hierarchical, radial, dagre) — critical for call graph analysis where parent-child relationships matter.
Add a layout selector (Force / Hierarchical / Dagre) with auto-switching based on graph density or user-defined call hierarchy depth.

The tree appears to be an abstraction (e.g., “daemon.* namespace” with counts) rather than a direct reflection of ELF section headers or symbol table entries — risks hiding low-level details REs need.
Hide advanced graph controls (Force, Hierarchy, Cluster) behind a “Show Advanced” toggle until the user has loaded at least one binary and interacted with the graph — reduce cognitive load early on.
Force-directed layout is active but not yet populated — in binary graphs, this often leads to chaotic edge crossings and node overlap without constraints.
Add toggleable columns for `Address`, `Size`, `Entropy`, and `Tags`, with persistent layout saving.
“Show Namespaces” and “Show Top Hubs” are abstract; no indication of how they map to actual sections, symbols, or memory layout — forces guesswork.
No visible loading state or animation when graph populates — feels static and unresponsive. Beginner won’t know if action succeeded or if tool is frozen.
Advanced features like “Force”, “Hierarchy”, “Cluster”, and “Viewport” controls are exposed immediately without tooltips or progressive disclosure — could intimidate or mislead new users.
Column widths are fixed and not resizable — long feature names or namespace paths get truncated, reducing readability.
Add a “Getting Started” overlay or tour on first load: Show a sample graph with annotations explaining nodes, edges, and interaction modes (click, double-click, right-click).

weaknesses: Empty central graph viewport on first load is confusing — no visual feedback or “no nodes selected” prompt; feels broken or incomplete.
suggestions: “+ INGEST BINARY” button is buried; no onboarding or “Start Here” cue for new users — reduces discoverability.
Add a “Welcome Graph” or “Sample Analysis” on first load showing a minimal connected graph with tooltips explaining interactions (click, double-click, right-click).
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7.5/10

3. Strengths  
- Clear project and binary selection UI with visual highlighting (e.g., “droid” selected in green) — reduces cognitive load for analysts.  
- Rich metadata panel at bottom (T0/T1/T2, confidence, namespaces, features) provides immediate context for triage — useful for agentic pipelines.  
- Integration hooks visible (e.g., “analyze-elf + treesitter”) suggest extensibility for automated workflows — aligns with agentic RE philosophy.

4. Weaknesses  
- Empty central graph viewport on first load is confusing — no visual feedback or “no nodes selected” prompt; feels broken or incomplete.  
- Suggested queries in right panel are helpful but lack execution context — no “Run Query” button or visual mapping to graph nodes.  
- “+ INGEST BINARY” button is buried; no onboarding or “Start Here” cue for new users — reduces discoverability.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Welcome Graph” or “Sample Analysis” on first load showing a minimal connected graph with tooltips explaining interactions (click, double-click, right-click).  
- Make suggested queries actionable — add “▶ Run” buttons that auto-populate the graph and highlight relevant nodes, bridging analysis intent to visualization.

weaknesses: Force-directed layout is active but not yet populated — in binary graphs, this often leads to chaotic edge crossings and node overlap without constraints.
No visible toggle between layout algorithms (hierarchical, radial, dagre) — critical for call graph analysis where parent-child relationships matter.
suggestions: Real-time stats (334 features, 755 edges) and namespace breakdowns provide immediate context for scale and complexity.
Add a layout selector (Force / Hierarchical / Dagre) with auto-switching based on graph density or user-defined call hierarchy depth.
Introduce edge bundling and node clustering by namespace or function type to reduce visual clutter and improve readability of large graphs.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths  
- The interface cleanly separates binary ingestion, namespace filtering, and feature visualization — excellent for iterative exploration.  
- Suggested queries (e.g., “What does request_permission do?”) demonstrate agentic guidance, reducing cognitive load for analysts.  
- Real-time stats (334 features, 755 edges) and namespace breakdowns provide immediate context for scale and complexity.

4. Weaknesses  
- Force-directed layout is active but not yet populated — in binary graphs, this often leads to chaotic edge crossings and node overlap without constraints.  
- No visible toggle between layout algorithms (hierarchical, radial, dagre) — critical for call graph analysis where parent-child relationships matter.  
- Layout stability cannot be assessed yet, but absence of “freeze layout” or “pin node” controls suggests potential instability during interaction.

5. What you would change  
- Add a layout selector (Force / Hierarchical / Dagre) with auto-switching based on graph density or user-defined call hierarchy depth.  
- Introduce edge bundling and node clustering by namespace or function type to reduce visual clutter and improve readability of large graphs.

- Aggregated metrics (e.g., “334 features · 3 namespaces · 6 state machines”) provide immediate context on scale and complexity — useful for triage.

reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10  

**Strengths**  
- Clear categorization of features by type (e.g., `cli_command`, `event`) with confidence scores — useful for triaging high-value functions during analysis.  
- Integration of namespace context (`daemon.*`, `droid.*`, `tunnel.*`) aids in understanding feature scope and potential attack surface boundaries.  
- Suggested queries panel provides guided exploration — valuable for junior analysts or when pivoting across complex binaries.  

**Weaknesses**  
- Missing critical RE columns: no visible address (VA/RVA), size, or entropy/entropy-based tags — essential for function boundary analysis and obfuscation detection.  
- No visible column customization or export capability — limits workflow integration with external tools (e.g., IDA, Ghidra, custom scripts).  
- Column widths are fixed and not resizable — long feature names or namespace paths get truncated, reducing readability.  

**What you would change**  
- Add toggleable columns for `Address`, `Size`, `Entropy`, and `Tags`, with persistent layout saving.  
- Introduce a “Export to CSV/JSON” button with column selection and filtering persistence — critical for batch analysis and correlation with other datasets.

reviews.fullText:
1. FAIL  
2. Score: 4/10

3. Strengths  
- Clean, modern UI with logical panel separation (binaries list, graph view, analysis sidebar) — good for exploratory navigation.  
- Suggested queries in the ANALYSIS panel show intent to guide users toward meaningful reverse engineering questions (e.g., “How do daemon.* and droid.* interact?”).  
- Metadata summary at bottom (T0/T1/T2, namespaces, features, edges) provides quick statistical context — useful for triage.

4. Weaknesses  
- No visible assembly view whatsoever — critical omission for any professional RE tool. No mnemonics, no operands, no hex addresses, no opcode bytes.  
- No branch target visualization or control flow graph — the “graph” appears to be a high-level feature/namespace dependency map, not a CFG.  
- Symbolic resolution and library call identification are not shown — no indication of imported/exported symbols, libc calls, or PLT/GOT entries.

5. What you would change  
- Add a disassembly pane (Intel syntax by default) with:  
  • Opcode bytes on left, mnemonic + operands on right  
  • Hex addresses with symbolic names (e.g., `call printf@plt`)  
  • Cross-references and jump arrows visually connecting branches  
- Integrate a CFG view toggle that overlays assembly with control flow edges — essential for understanding logic flow in obfuscated or packed binaries.

This tool is currently a “binary feature explorer,” not a reverse engineering environment. It’s useful for high-level triage — but not for actual disassembly or code analysis. For that, you still need IDA, Ghidra, or x64dbg.

reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 6/10  

3. Strengths  
- Clean, dark-themed UI with clear visual hierarchy — reduces cognitive load for users already familiar with RE tools.  
- Suggested queries panel offers concrete starting points (“How do daemon.* namespace and droid.* namespace interact?”), which is helpful for guided exploration.  
- Feature-rich metadata panel (namespaces, connections, stats) provides deep context once the user knows what to look for.  

4. Weaknesses  
- Empty graph on first load is confusing — no visual feedback or “getting started” prompt to explain what the user should do next.  
- No onboarding tour, tooltips, or “CTF Beginner Mode” toggle — critical for educational accessibility.  
- Suggested queries are advanced and assume prior knowledge (e.g., “trace the B:Connection state machine”) — not scaffolded for novices.  

5. What you would change  
- Add a “First Steps” modal or tooltip chain on first load: “Click a binary → See graph → Right-click nodes for context → Try a suggested query.”  
- Introduce a “Beginner Mode” toggle that simplifies the UI, highlights key features, and replaces advanced queries with “What is this binary doing?” or “Find the main function.”  

> Would I feature this in a YouTube tutorial?  
> **Yes — but only after adding beginner scaffolding.** The tool has strong bones for teaching, but right now it’s like handing a beginner a disassembler without a “Hello World” example. With minor UX tweaks, this could be a killer educational tool for my channel.

weaknesses: No visible loading state or animation when graph populates — feels static and unresponsive. Beginner won’t know if action succeeded or if tool is frozen.
“Click to select node” instructions are buried in header; no visual cue or tooltip to explain interaction model.
“6/334 in scene” is cryptic — doesn’t explain why only 6 are shown or how to reveal more. No “load more” or “expand all” affordance.
suggestions: Metadata summary at bottom (features, edges, namespaces) gives immediate context — useful for triage.
Add a subtle “spinning node” or “pulse animation” on the selected binary (e.g., “droid”) when clicked, followed by a radial “bloom” of connected nodes to visually tie action to result.
Replace “6/334 in scene” with “Showing 6 of 334 features — click to expand full graph” and add a “+ Load All” button near the viewport controls.

1. FAIL  
2. Score: 5/10

3. Strengths  
- Clean, dark-themed UI with high contrast for readability — excellent for long RE sessions.  
- Suggested queries panel is pedagogically strong; guides beginners toward meaningful exploration (e.g., “What does request_permission do?”).  
- Metadata summary at bottom (features, edges, namespaces) gives immediate context — useful for triage.

4. Weaknesses  
- No visible loading state or animation when graph populates — feels static and unresponsive. Beginner won’t know if action succeeded or if tool is frozen.  
- “Click to select node” instructions are buried in header; no visual cue or tooltip to explain interaction model.  
- “6/334 in scene” is cryptic — doesn’t explain why only 6 are shown or how to reveal more. No “load more” or “expand all” affordance.

5. What you would change  
- Add a subtle “spinning node” or “pulse animation” on the selected binary (e.g., “droid”) when clicked, followed by a radial “bloom” of connected nodes to visually tie action to result.  
- Replace “6/334 in scene” with “Showing 6 of 334 features — click to expand full graph” and add a “+ Load All” button near the viewport controls.

weaknesses: The central graph canvas is empty on first load — no visual scaffolding or “hello world” example to demonstrate what a populated graph looks like. This risks confusion for beginners.
“ANALYSIS” tab is active by default, but the graph is empty — no indication that the user must first select a binary or trigger a render. This breaks the “overview → detail” flow.
Advanced features like “Force”, “Hierarchy”, “Cluster”, and “Viewport” controls are exposed immediately without tooltips or progressive disclosure — could intimidate or mislead new users.
suggestions: Add a “Getting Started” overlay or tour on first load: Show a sample graph with annotations explaining nodes, edges, and interaction modes (click, double-click, right-click).
Hide advanced graph controls (Force, Hierarchy, Cluster) behind a “Show Advanced” toggle until the user has loaded at least one binary and interacted with the graph — reduce cognitive load early on.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths  
- Clear visual hierarchy: The left sidebar (projects → binaries → ingest) provides a logical entry point for newcomers to select and load binaries.  
- Contextual metadata panel (bottom) gives immediate stats (features, edges, namespaces) — useful for orienting users before diving into the graph.  
- Suggested queries in the right panel act as guided discovery prompts, nudging users toward meaningful questions without overwhelming them.

4. Weaknesses  
- The central graph canvas is empty on first load — no visual scaffolding or “hello world” example to demonstrate what a populated graph looks like. This risks confusion for beginners.  
- “ANALYSIS” tab is active by default, but the graph is empty — no indication that the user must first select a binary or trigger a render. This breaks the “overview → detail” flow.  
- Advanced features like “Force”, “Hierarchy”, “Cluster”, and “Viewport” controls are exposed immediately without tooltips or progressive disclosure — could intimidate or mislead new users.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Getting Started” overlay or tour on first load: Show a sample graph with annotations explaining nodes, edges, and interaction modes (click, double-click, right-click).  
- Hide advanced graph controls (Force, Hierarchy, Cluster) behind a “Show Advanced” toggle until the user has loaded at least one binary and interacted with the graph — reduce cognitive load early on.

weaknesses: No explicit “Find Vulnerable Functions” or “Highlight Input Handlers” toggle — forces manual exploration even for common patterns (e.g., strcpy, recv, fopen).
“Start Exploring” screen is empty by default — no auto-highlighted risky functions or entry points (main, signal handlers, network binds) to jumpstart analysis.
No visual indicators for dangerous patterns (e.g., buffer ops, unvalidated args, hardcoded creds) — misses opportunity to flag potential bugs proactively.
suggestions: Add a “VULN HINTS” toggle that overlays icons or colors on functions matching known dangerous patterns (e.g., strcpy, system, recv with no length check).
Auto-load and highlight the “main” function + any network-related functions (bind, listen, accept) on first load — gives immediate attack surface context without clicking “Show Namespaces.”
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths  
- Clear visual separation of namespaces (daemon.*, droid.*, tunnel.*) helps segment attack surface by subsystem — useful for isolating network, auth, or IPC handlers.  
- “Top Features by Connections” highlights high-traffic or central functions (e.g., add_mcp_server, archive_session) — a good heuristic for finding entry points or critical logic.  
- Suggested queries (e.g., “What is the attack surface of daemon.* namespace?”) guide beginners and reduce cognitive load during time-constrained CTFs.

4. Weaknesses  
- No explicit “Find Vulnerable Functions” or “Highlight Input Handlers” toggle — forces manual exploration even for common patterns (e.g., strcpy, recv, fopen).  
- “Start Exploring” screen is empty by default — no auto-highlighted risky functions or entry points (main, signal handlers, network binds) to jumpstart analysis.  
- No visual indicators for dangerous patterns (e.g., buffer ops, unvalidated args, hardcoded creds) — misses opportunity to flag potential bugs proactively.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “VULN HINTS” toggle that overlays icons or colors on functions matching known dangerous patterns (e.g., strcpy, system, recv with no length check).  
- Auto-load and highlight the “main” function + any network-related functions (bind, listen, accept) on first load — gives immediate attack surface context without clicking “Show Namespaces.”

weaknesses: No Python pseudocode is visible — the core requirement of this evaluation is unmet. The interface shows no function-level logic translation.
“SOURCE PATTERNS (0 available)” implies the tool hasn’t extracted or rendered any function logic at all — critical for educational use.
The UI prioritizes graph topology over code semantics; without pseudocode, it’s useless for teaching binary logic to Python-savvy learners.
suggestions: Visual indicators (T0/T1/T2, confidence %) provide quick risk/complexity heuristics — helpful for prioritizing analysis.
Add a “Pseudocode View” toggle that renders each selected function as Python-like pseudocode with inline comments explaining assembly idioms (e.g., “# equivalent to cmp eax, 0; jz label”).
Integrate a “Teach Me” mode that auto-generates annotated pseudocode for top features (e.g., “request_permission”) with links to CTF-style explanations.
reviews.fullText:
1. FAIL  
2. Score: 4/10  

3. Strengths  
- The interface presents high-level metadata (namespaces, features, edges) in a structured, digestible format — useful for rapid triage.  
- Suggested queries in the ANALYSIS panel are pedagogically sound, guiding users toward meaningful exploration (e.g., “What does request_permission do?”).  
- Visual indicators (T0/T1/T2, confidence %) provide quick risk/complexity heuristics — helpful for prioritizing analysis.  

4. Weaknesses  
- No Python pseudocode is visible — the core requirement of this evaluation is unmet. The interface shows no function-level logic translation.  
- “SOURCE PATTERNS (0 available)” implies the tool hasn’t extracted or rendered any function logic at all — critical for educational use.  
- The UI prioritizes graph topology over code semantics; without pseudocode, it’s useless for teaching binary logic to Python-savvy learners.  

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Pseudocode View” toggle that renders each selected function as Python-like pseudocode with inline comments explaining assembly idioms (e.g., “# equivalent to cmp eax, 0; jz label”).  
- Integrate a “Teach Me” mode that auto-generates annotated pseudocode for top features (e.g., “request_permission”) with links to CTF-style explanations.
